claim: Text-based reviews (manual or LLM-assisted) remain susceptible to confirmation bias, hallucination, and insufficient self-validation.

assumptions.text: Confirmation bias in LLM-assisted reviews is empirically demonstrated, not merely theoretical.
The susceptibility applies broadly across review domains, not only to specific task types.

claim: None of the existing approaches (formal inspections, Constitutional AI, multi-agent debate) combine all four features in a single implementable protocol: (i) atomic claim extraction, (ii) mandatory adversarial contradiction, (iii) evidence binding, (iv) formal gate rule.

assumptions.text: The four features are the correct and exhaustive set of distinguishing features.
The related-work survey is comprehensive enough to support a gap claim.

claim: Table 1 correctly characterizes Fagan inspection as having role separation and runtime operation, partial mandatory contradiction, but lacking evidence binding, auditable finding artifacts, formal gate rule, and authority grounding.

assumptions.text: Fagan's moderator role and rework/follow-up phases do not constitute evidence binding or formal gating.

claim: Constitutional AI is a single-model approach that does not structurally prevent correlated blind spots, lacks auditable persistent finding artifacts, and has no formal gate mechanism.

assumptions.text: CAI as described in Bai et al. 2022 cannot be extended to multi-model deployment to overcome these limitations.

claim: Multi-agent debate (Du et al.) lacks an explicit audit role, evidence binding against external sources, and a formal severity-based gate.

assumptions.text: The multi-agent debate paradigm has not been extended by subsequent work to include these features.

claim: Multi-agent orchestration frameworks (AutoGen, CrewAI, LangGraph) are complementary infrastructure layers that do not prescribe contradiction, audit, or gating semantics.

assumptions.text: No existing configuration or template in these frameworks implements all four IM features out of the box.

claim: The Ipcha Score IS = 1 − cos(embed(Ap), embed(Af)) is a valid proxy indicator of semantic displacement between Proponent and Auditor outputs.

assumptions.text: Cosine distance in embedding space is a meaningful proxy for semantic divergence in review quality assessment.
text-embedding-3-large captures review-relevant semantic differences faithfully.

claim: Low IS (< 0.15) indicates robust original or weak critique; mid-range (0.15–0.45) indicates normal dialectical refinement; high IS (> 0.45) indicates substantial correction pressure. Thresholds are preliminary from early pilot observations.

assumptions.text: The threshold values have some empirical basis from pilot observations.
The bands are reasonably stable across different artifact types.

claim: The IS has two known failure modes: false positive (high score, low correction from paraphrasing) and false negative (low score, high correction from single critical change). IS_w mitigates the false-negative mode.

assumptions.text: These are the most significant failure modes; no other critical failure modes exist.

claim: Three structural properties distinguish IM from prompt chains: mandatory execution, persistent auditable artifacts, and formal gate mechanism.

assumptions.text: These properties cannot be trivially replicated by well-configured prompt chain frameworks.

claim: IM is designed with domain-agnostic abstractions (Claim, Evidence, Finding, Gate Rule); domain-agnosticity is a design goal, not yet empirically validated.

assumptions.text: Abstract formal structures are sufficient for domain portability without domain-specific prompt/authority customization.

claim: The IM approach is inspired by fault tolerance in distributed systems; the analogy is structural, not formal — IM does not provide BFT or provable safety/liveness guarantees.
Correlated model failures are a core limitation when all roles share a foundation model. Mitigations include model diversification, authority grounding, and periodic human audit.

assumptions.text: Authority grounding with external rule sets effectively compensates for shared model blind spots.

claim: The pilot case study applied IM to a production design specification (Persona Evaluation v2) and surfaced 2 critical and 4 high-severity findings that were subsequently resolved.

assumptions.text: The pilot data accurately represents a genuine protocol application, not a cherry-picked or retroactively constructed example.

claim: The pilot workflow used two providers (Gemini, Kimi) with four analytical lenses each, consuming 77,773 tokens at $0.198 total cost in 2.8 minutes.

assumptions.text: The reported metrics are accurate and reproducible.

claim: Cross-model consensus findings (CM-01 through CM-06) were independently identified by two or more provider-lens combinations and all six were subsequently validated as genuine vulnerabilities.

assumptions.text: Independence of provider-lens combinations is genuine (no shared context leaking between lenses).
'Validated as genuine vulnerabilities' implies an independent validation step beyond the protocol itself.

claim: The pilot gate decision was G(F) = 0 (BLOCK), with an adversarial robustness score of 25/100. All critical and high findings were resolved in the specification revision.

assumptions.text: The hardening changes (Table, lines 576–588) genuinely resolve the identified vulnerabilities rather than merely addressing their surface manifestations.

claim: Under German patent law (PatG §3) and EPC Art. 54, public availability is novelty-destroying. Zenodo metadata is CC0-licensed and unsuitable for trade secrets.

assumptions.text: The IP analysis correctly applies to academic preprint publication contexts.

claim: The paper contains a Zenodo-ready artifact manifest satisfying USENIX Security 2026 and ACM CCS 2026 artifact requirements.

assumptions.text: A manifest of placeholder files constitutes readiness for venue requirements.

claim: The protocol's value scales with artifact complexity: at $0.20 and 2.8 minutes, it surfaced findings that would have required multiple expert reviewers and substantially more time manually.

assumptions.text: The comparison with manual expert review time is reasonable, not just asserted.
A single pilot is sufficient to support a scaling claim.
